title: Exploring Virtual Reality in Game Development Education: Student Perspectives on Learning
summary: This study explores how Virtual Reality (VR) influences student learning and engagement in higher-education computer programming classrooms. Using an interpretivist, phenomenological approach, five third-year computer science students at a South African university were interviewed about their experiences with VR-based learning. Thematic analysis revealed six key themes: access and hardware limitations, immersion and emotional influence, learning curves and problem-solving, teamwork and collaboration, curriculum gaps, and impacts on programming mindset. Findings indicate that VR fosters heightened immersion, motivation, and problem-solving skills, aligning with Dewey's Learning by Doing and Csikszentmihalyi's Flow Theory. However, challenges such as motion sickness, steep learning curves, and insufficient institutional support hinder effective integration. The study concludes that VR has strong potential to enhance programming education but requires careful, theory-informed implementation.
